caveats: a: The occupation mix is specific to NAICS 238140 but dates to May 2022 and excludes self-employed workers.
a: Occupation shares are employment-weighted rather than wage- and task-weighted.
a: The estimate measures technically exposed current tasks, not implementation or robotics.
rho: Survey expectations do not measure realized implementation.
rho: The survey combines robotics and AI and spans construction segments and firm sizes.
rho: Implementation may differ sharply between restoration specialists, commercial block contractors, and residential stucco firms.
e: No source measures lens eligibility directly among masonry firms in the supplied EBITDA band.
e: The NAICS code mixes commercial block, residential veneer, stucco, stone, and restoration businesses with different transferability.
e: The supplied firm count is itself an estimate.
s5: The Census age distribution covers responding owners across employer industries and uses 2018 reference data.
s5: BizBuySell data are voluntarily reported and cover broad building and construction businesses, not masonry alone.
s5: The estimate excludes closures and ownership events that are not qualifying control transfers.
q: The contract source is a construction software vendor, not a representative government survey.
q: The cited fixed-price share is cross-trade and not masonry-specific.
q: Retention varies with local bid density, bonding requirements, builder concentration, specialty reputation, and repair versus new-construction mix.
d5: Occupational employment is an imperfect proxy for service demand and spans employers outside NAICS 238140.
d5: The BLS projection is ten-year and the five-year conversion assumes a smooth path.
d5: Masonry demand is cyclical, regional, and sensitive to architectural preferences and relative material cost.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for NAICS 238140.
o: Prefabrication and product substitution may advance faster in repetitive new construction than in repair, restoration, or custom stone work.
o: This primitive does not count task automation already reflected in a and rho.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based subcontract or direct-owner revenue, commonly bid as a fixed-price or lump-sum scope and billed by percentage of completion; some repair, restoration, and uncertain-scope work uses time-and-materials or unit-price terms. Recurrence comes through repeat builders, general contractors, institutions, and property portfolios rather than subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive masonry crews, material manufacturers and distributors without contracted installation, labor-only shells without transferable customer or supervisory infrastructure, unrelated poured-concrete specialists, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market contractors performing bricklaying, block laying, stone setting, stucco, masonry foundations, pointing, cleaning, caulking, restoration, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs for builders, general contractors, institutions, commercial owners, and homeowners.

a.bridge: May 2022 OEWS directly measures the NAICS 238140 employee occupation mix but does not measure task-level AI exposure and excludes self-employed workers. It reports 80.85% of employment in construction/extraction occupations, versus 6.48% in office/administrative support, 4.25% in management, and 2.89% in business/financial operations; supervisors are 6.62%, cost estimators 1.37%, and construction managers 1.79%. The estimate applies high exposure only to portions of estimating, project management, bookkeeping, bidding, scheduling, and document handling, and low exposure to physical masonry. Excluding self-employed owner-operators may overstate exposed back-office specialization, while employment rather than wage weighting understates the influence of higher-paid office and management roles; the net direction is uncertain.
caveats: The occupation mix is specific to NAICS 238140 but dates to May 2022 and excludes self-employed workers.
Occupation shares are employment-weighted rather than wage- and task-weighted.
The estimate measures technically exposed current tasks, not implementation or robotics.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary across at least 30 masonry contractors, separating estimating, takeoff, scheduling, project documentation, supervision, layout, material handling, laying, finishing, and repair work, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Most compensation supports material handling, mortar preparation, layout, laying and finishing units, scaffolding, equipment operation, supervision, and field adjustment. Current AI can substitute parts of the estimating, coordination, documentation, and administrative layer, but little of the core physical craft.

d5.bridge: BLS projects overall masonry-worker employment from 294,300 in 2024 to 300,000 in 2034, a 2% ten-year increase; brickmasons and blockmasons rise 3%, stonemasons decline 3%, and terrazzo workers decline 11%. Applying roughly half of the overall change gives a central five-year anchor near 1.01. Employment is not constant-price, constant-quality demand and the occupational group includes cement masons outside NAICS 238140, so the range is widened for productivity, trade mix, and industry-population mismatch. Because offsite panels can lower onsite employment without proportionally lowering end-customer masonry quantity, employment may understate demand; material substitution can push the other way.
caveats: Occupational employment is an imperfect proxy for service demand and spans employers outside NAICS 238140.
The BLS projection is ten-year and the five-year conversion assumes a smooth path.
Masonry demand is cyclical, regional, and sensitive to architectural preferences and relative material cost.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar forecast of installed U.S. brick, block, stone, and stucco volume by new construction, repair, restoration, and end market, adjusted for installation productivity and offsite panels, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Building and road construction, repair, restoration, and the continuing use of brick and stone support the service basket, while higher-cost natural stone, product substitution, polished concrete, prefabricated panels, and construction cyclicality limit growth.

caveats: No source measures lens eligibility directly among masonry firms in the supplied EBITDA band.
The NAICS code mixes commercial block, residential veneer, stucco, stone, and restoration businesses with different transferability.
The supplied firm count is itself an estimate.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of state masonry-contractor licenses, payroll records, customer concentration, and 100 confidential information memoranda for businesses in the supplied earnings band would measure captive status, owner dependence, and transferable operating infrastructure.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition consists mainly of contracted external work and spans new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs, so most firms in the supplied earnings band should fit the lens. The range removes captive crews, labor-only shells, businesses dependent on a departing owner's personal production or license, and firms lacking transferable customers, supervision, or safety systems.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23814 ($8,889,909,891) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23814 ($31,484,584,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23814. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $229,518 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238140); class <5: 12,785 firms, avg revenue/firm $383,220 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,504 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,771 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,390,414 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $110,677 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,439 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,962,960 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,852 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,198 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,739,080 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $695,631 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 144 firms, avg revenue/firm $33,152,611 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,638,948 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $146,599,591 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,669,327 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 144

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for NAICS 238140.
Prefabrication and product substitution may advance faster in repetitive new construction than in repair, restoration, or custom stone work.
This primitive does not count task automation already reflected in a and rho.
o.change_evidence: Builder and architect procurement data measuring masonry square footage delivered by independent subcontractors versus self-performed crews, panel manufacturers, alternative cladding systems, and robotic installation, segmented by end market over five years, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Masonry remains a strenuous onsite trade requiring material preparation, precise layout, laying, cutting, finishing, scaffolding, safety control, coordination, inspection response, and warranty accountability. Prefabricated panels and alternative products can shift some installation to manufacturers or other trades, while large builders may self-perform, but most remaining masonry quantity still needs an accountable contractor rather than software alone.

q.bridge: A construction subcontractor billing provider says fixed-price work is the most common contract form between subcontractors and general contractors, cites 80% fixed-price usage among 38,000 projects entered in another construction-software dataset during 2018-2020, and explains that contractors can keep gains when labor hours come in below estimate. This supports near-term retention within signed projects but does not measure discounted five-year pass-through for masonry. The transformation assumes frequent rebidding, comparable scopes, and customer concentration pass much of a visible gain through over time. Software-recorded projects may overrepresent organized residential builders, making the fixed-price share potentially higher than for restoration and uncertain-condition masonry work.
caveats: The contract source is a construction software vendor, not a representative government survey.
The cited fixed-price share is cross-trade and not masonry-specific.
Retention varies with local bid density, bonding requirements, builder concentration, specialty reputation, and repair versus new-construction mix.
q.change_evidence: Matched pre/post automation bid and job-cost data from at least 20 masonry contractors, including contract type, win rate, gross margin, labor variance, rework, change orders, and repeat-customer pricing, would materially move the estimate.
q.rationale: A fixed-price scope preserves savings on the awarded job, especially when better takeoffs, scheduling, and documentation reduce hours or leakage. General contractors and owners rebid new scopes, compare quotes, demand savings, and shift work among subcontractors, so competitive repricing erodes retention across five years.

rho.bridge: The 2025 AGC-NCCER survey covers construction firms broadly, not masonry contractors or the exposed task pool. It reports 45% expecting robotics and AI to automate manual or error-prone tasks over five years and 44% expecting safer or more productive jobs; it also reports that fewer than half of firms with revenue of $50 million or less adopted certain modern recruiting approaches. These attitudes anchor a partial five-year implementation rate, discounted for small-contractor data fragmentation, bespoke field conditions, quality control, and limited change capacity. Because the broad survey includes larger and more technology-capable firms, it likely overstates implementation for many masonry contractors.
caveats: Survey expectations do not measure realized implementation.
The survey combines robotics and AI and spans construction segments and firm sizes.
Implementation may differ sharply between restoration specialists, commercial block contractors, and residential stucco firms.
rho.change_evidence: Independent vendor telemetry or a longitudinal Mason Contractors Association of America survey showing task-level AI deployment, user retention, hours avoided, rework, and error rates by firm size would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Estimating, plan review, billing, safety documentation, submittals, schedule coordination, and customer communication are reachable with current tools. Fragmented plans, variable existing conditions, owner knowledge, field connectivity, integration cost, and the need to verify outputs constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were at least 55 in 2018, while BizBuySell reports 3,142 sold building-and-construction listings analyzed and annual transaction observations from 2021 through 2025. Neither source supplies a masonry-specific eligible-firm denominator or isolates qualifying control transfers. The estimate translates broad age pressure and demonstrated construction deal flow into a five-year probability and discounts for closures, family or employee succession, internal reorganizations, and businesses too owner-dependent to sell. The all-industry age statistic may misstate masonry owner age; broker data may omit private transactions but overrepresent smaller firms below the lens.
caveats: The Census age distribution covers responding owners across employer industries and uses 2018 reference data.
BizBuySell data are voluntarily reported and cover broad building and construction businesses, not masonry alone.
The estimate excludes closures and ownership events that are not qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched cohort using contractor-license ownership changes, PitchBook/Capital IQ control deals, SBA 7(a) acquisition loans, ESOP records, and broker closed-deal files filtered to NAICS 238140 and the supplied earnings band would materially change this range.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership and observed construction-business sales support meaningful transfer incidence, but an owner's age does not establish sale intent or transferability. Craft reputation, licensing, bonding, customer ties, and foreman depth can make a masonry business either durable or inseparable from its owner.

principal_driver: High labor intensity makes better estimating, project control, and administration economically relevant even though the physical craft remains human.
principal_weakness: AI reaches only a small share of current work, while competitive rebidding and prefabricated alternatives constrain retained benefits.

caveats: S1 is a reachable but non-supporting citation: its exact URL does not expose the claimed 238140 definition. This is non-material because e and o are already labeled ESTIMATE, and correcting the citation or removing its decorative support would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The rho base translates broad construction-firm expectations about combined robotics and AI into a masonry implementation rate. The transformation and likely upward bias are disclosed, but the survey measures attitudes rather than deployment or realized task substitution.

The s5 base is weakly identified by an all-industry owner-age statistic and broad brokered construction transactions, neither of which supplies a masonry-specific eligible-firm denominator or qualifying-control-transfer rate; the packet discloses this limitation and keeps the input at PROXY.

The q base extrapolates from vendor-authored, cross-trade contract commentary and software-recorded projects to discounted five-year pass-through in masonry. The source supports near-term fixed-price retention mechanics, not the selected long-run retention value, and the packet states that bridge explicitly.

sources_audited.note: The cited URL opens the Census NAICS search page with a sector-23 query but does not display the 238140 definition or the listed masonry activities. The underlying definition is plausible, but this exact cited page does not substantiate it.

The May 2022 OEWS page is specific to NAICS 238140 and reports 143,410 employees plus the cited occupation shares, including management, business/financial, office/administrative, construction/extraction, supervisors, estimators, and construction managers.

The BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook reports the cited 2024 and 2034 employment levels, overall and detailed growth rates, physical duties and conditions, and the stated demand supports and substitution pressures.

The 2025 AGC-NCCER survey reports the cited 45% and 44% expectations for robotics and AI over five years, describes lower adoption of selected recruiting approaches among firms with no more than $50 million in revenue, and identifies its broad construction-firm sample.
The Census graphic reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses age 55 or older and clearly identifies the 2019 Annual Business Survey, 2018 data year, and population limitation.

The BizBuySell page reports 3,142 analyzed sold listings, annual 2021-2025 transaction observations, and that the data cover broad building and construction businesses sold on and reported to BizBuySell.

The Siteline article describes the four contract forms, calls fixed-price the most common between subcontractors and general contractors, cites 80% of 38,000 CoConstruct-recorded projects in 2018-2020, and explains the contractor's ability to retain within-project cost savings.

sources.supports: Defines masonry contractors as establishments primarily engaged in masonry work, stone setting, bricklaying, and other stone work, including new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs, with examples including block laying, pointing, cleaning, caulking, stucco, and masonry foundations.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS: 238140 Masonry Contractors

sources.supports: Reports 143,410 employees and the industry-specific occupation mix: 80.85% construction/extraction, 6.48% office/administrative support, 4.25% management, and 2.89% business/financial operations, including supervisors, estimators, construction managers, brickmasons, stonemasons, laborers, and helpers.
sources.title: May 2022 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 238140

sources.supports: Describes masonry duties and strenuous onsite conditions; reports 294,300 jobs in 2024, 300,000 projected in 2034, 2% overall growth, and detailed projections; says new building and road construction support work while prefabricated panels, high natural-stone cost, and polished-concrete substitution constrain demand.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Masonry Workers

sources.supports: AGC-NCCER construction survey reports 45% expect robotics and AI to automate manual or error-prone tasks over five years, 44% expect safer or more productive jobs, and smaller firms lag some modernization approaches; it also documents widespread craft and salaried hiring difficulty.
sources.title: 2025 Workforce Survey Analysis

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey, using 2018 data, and states the population limitations.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Reports 3,142 sold building-and-construction listings analyzed, annual transaction pricing from 2021 through 2025, and explains that the sample is based on construction businesses sold on and reported to BizBuySell.
sources.title: Construction Business Valuation Benchmarks

sources.supports: Explains fixed-price, time-and-materials, cost-plus, and unit-price subcontract billing; says fixed price is most common between subcontractors and GCs, cites 80% fixed-price usage among 38,000 software-recorded projects, and notes contractors can retain gains from lower labor hours or material cost on fixed-price work.
sources.title: 4 Main Types of Construction Contracts and How to Bill for Each

title: Masonry Contractors
